female-45-iron-deficiency.name: Michael Rodriguez
female-45-iron-deficiency.chiefComplaint: I'm exhausted all the time and feel weak
female-45-iron-deficiency.variantDescription: Iron deficiency anemia from heavy menstrual bleeding

missedConsequences.Iron Deficiency Anemia: Failure to identify source leads to ongoing blood loss. In postmenopausal women or men, must rule out GI malignancy.
missedConsequences.Colon Cancer: Iron deficiency in men or postmenopausal women requires GI workup - cancer until proven otherwise.
missedConsequences.B12 Deficiency: Missed B12 deficiency leads to irreversible neurological damage if untreated.

commonPitfalls: Not determining the TYPE of anemia (microcytic vs macrocytic vs normocytic)
Treating iron deficiency without finding the source of blood loss
Missing GI malignancy as cause of iron deficiency in men/postmenopausal women
Forgetting to check reticulocyte count to assess marrow response
Not checking B12/folate in macrocytic anemia
Missing hemolysis (check LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin)

mcqs.question: A 45-year-old woman has Hgb 9.2, MCV 72 (low), ferritin 8 (low), TIBC 450 (high). What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.explanation: ACD has low iron AND low TIBC (both decreased). Ferritin is normal or high. This pattern shows low ferritin and HIGH TIBC = iron deficiency.
Classic iron deficiency: Microcytic (low MCV), low ferritin (reflects iron stores), HIGH TIBC (body trying to absorb more iron). Now find the source!
Thalassemia has low MCV but normal iron studies. RDW is normal (uniform small cells). Mentzer index (MCV/RBC) <13 suggests thalassemia.
Sideroblastic has high iron and ferritin with ringed sideroblasts on marrow biopsy. This pattern is classic iron deficiency.

mcqs.question: A 65-year-old man is found to have iron deficiency anemia. He denies any bleeding. What is the MOST important next step?

options.text: Start oral iron supplementation and recheck in 3 months
options.explanation: NEVER just treat iron deficiency in a man or postmenopausal woman without finding the source. GI malignancy is a major concern.
Iron deficiency in men or postmenopausal women = GI blood loss until proven otherwise. Must scope to rule out colon cancer, gastric cancer, or other GI pathology.
Fecal occult blood testing is not sensitive enough. Even if negative, still need endoscopy. A negative FOBT does not rule out GI malignancy.
Marrow biopsy rarely needed for iron deficiency - diagnosis made by iron studies. Need to find SOURCE of blood loss first.

allDiagnoses.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Microcytic anemia, low ferritin, high TIBC. Heavy menstrual bleeding provides the source.

allDiagnoses.name: Menorrhagia (Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)
allDiagnoses.rationale: History of heavy periods is the likely source of chronic blood loss causing iron deficiency.

allDiagnoses.name: Colon Cancer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must consider in any iron deficiency, especially postmenopausal women. This premenopausal woman with menorrhagia has clear source, but still consider if not responding to treatment.

allDiagnoses.name: Other GI Blood Loss
allDiagnoses.rationale: Peptic ulcer, gastritis, angiodysplasia can cause occult blood loss. Consider if menstrual loss doesn't fully explain anemia.

allDiagnoses.name: Celiac Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause iron deficiency from malabsorption. Consider if iron deficiency refractory to oral supplementation.

allDiagnoses.name: Thalassemia Trait
allDiagnoses.rationale: Also microcytic but iron studies are normal. Would expect very low MCV with relatively preserved Hgb.

allDiagnoses.name: Anemia of Chronic Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Normocytic or mildly microcytic, but iron studies show low iron WITH low TIBC (not high). Ferritin normal or elevated.

female-45-iron-deficiency.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been so tired for the past 6 months. I used to have energy to work out after work but now I can barely make it through the day. I feel weak, especially climbing stairs - I get winded. I've noticed I'm paler than usual and my nails seem more brittle. My periods have been really heavy for the past year or two - I'm going through a super tampon every 1-2 hours on my heavy days and I've been having accidents at night. I sometimes pass clots. My periods last about 7 days now. I've been craving ice - I chew it all day long, which is weird. I'm vegetarian but I eat eggs and dairy. No blood in my stool that I've noticed. No weight loss. No fevers. I take a multivitamin but nothing else."

historyQuestions.text: When did you start feeling this way?
historyQuestions.answer: It's been gradually getting worse over the past 6 months. At first I thought I was just stressed or not sleeping well, but it's definitely gotten worse over time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset over months is typical for iron deficiency from chronic blood loss

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about your fatigue
historyQuestions.answer: It's been getting worse over 6 months. I'm exhausted by the end of the day. I used to exercise but I don't have the energy anymore. Even climbing stairs leaves me winded.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset fatigue and dyspnea on exertion are classic anemia symptoms - decreased oxygen carrying capacity

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your periods
historyQuestions.answer: They've been really heavy for 1-2 years. On my heaviest days I'm going through a super tampon every 1-2 hours. I have to set alarms at night or I'll have accidents. I pass clots, some pretty large. My periods last about 7 days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: This is menorrhagia - changing pad/tampon hourly, clots, and night flooding indicate significant blood loss. Key source for iron deficiency in premenopausal women.

historyQuestions.text: Any unusual cravings?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes - I crave ice! I chew ice all day long. My coworkers think I'm crazy. I also sometimes want to chew on chalk.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pica (craving non-food items) and pagophagia (ice craving) are SPECIFIC for iron deficiency anemia

historyQuestions.text: Any blood in your stool or black stools?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I haven't noticed any blood. My stools look normal to me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to ask about GI blood loss. Absence of visible blood doesn't rule out occult loss, but menorrhagia is clear source here.

historyQuestions.text: What is your diet like?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm vegetarian - I don't eat meat or fish. I do eat eggs and dairy. I take a daily multivitamin.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vegetarian diet = less bioavailable iron (heme iron from meat is better absorbed). May contribute to difficulty replacing iron stores.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any changes in your appearance?
historyQuestions.answer: I look pale - people have commented on it. My nails seem brittle and kind of spoon-shaped. My hair is thinner.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pallor, koilonychia (spoon nails), and hair changes are signs of iron deficiency

historyQuestions.text: Any numbness, tingling, or balance problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Neurologic symptoms would suggest B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration). Absent here.

historyQuestions.text: Any weight loss or fevers?
historyQuestions.answer: No weight loss, no fevers. I've actually gained a few pounds because I'm too tired to exercise.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of constitutional symptoms argues against malignancy or chronic disease as primary cause

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a daily multivitamin and sometimes ibuprofen for cramps during my period. No prescription medications.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAID use can cause GI blood loss; multivitamin has minimal iron

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before starting iron supplementation

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: I have fibroids - my gynecologist found them a couple years ago. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Uterine fibroids are common cause of menorrhagia - explains the heavy bleeding

historyQuestions.text: Any prior surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my wisdom teeth. No abdominal surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No GI surgeries that would affect iron absorption

historyQuestions.text: Any blood disorders in your family?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother was anemic too, I think from heavy periods. No bleeding disorders or blood cancers.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of anemia; no inherited bleeding disorders

historyQuestions.text: When was your last Pap smear and gynecologic exam?
historyQuestions.answer: About a year ago. Everything was normal except they mentioned the fibroids.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recent gynecologic care; fibroids documented

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been told you were anemic before?
historyQuestions.answer: I was told my iron was 'a little low' a few years ago but I didn't do anything about it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: History of low iron suggests chronic problem now worsening

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your daily life?
historyQuestions.answer: I had to cut back my work hours. I can't exercise anymore. I'm barely keeping up with my kids. It's really affecting my quality of life.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant functional impairment - indicates severity and need for treatment

physicalExam.reasoning: Mild tachycardia at rest can occur with anemia. Vitals relatively stable indicating compensation.

physicalExam.finding: Pale-appearing female in no acute distress. Tired-appearing but alert and oriented.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor is classic finding in anemia - check conjunctivae, nail beds, palmar creases

physicalExam.finding: Conjunctival pallor present. Oral mucosa pale. No glossitis or angular cheilitis. No jaundice.
physicalExam.reasoning: Conjunctival and mucosal pallor indicate anemia. Glossitis/cheilitis can occur in severe iron or B12 deficiency. No jaundice argues against hemolysis.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm. Soft systolic flow murmur at LUSB. No gallops. Brisk capillary refill.
physicalExam.reasoning: Flow murmur is common in anemia - turbulent flow from hyperdynamic state due to low viscosity blood

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No masses. Normal bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: No organomegaly (would suggest hemolysis, malignancy, or infiltrative disease). No abdominal masses.

physicalExam.finding: Koilonychia (spooning) of several fingernails. Brittle nails with longitudinal ridging.
physicalExam.reasoning: Koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) is specific for iron deficiency anemia

physicalExam.finding: Normal strength, sensation, and reflexes. No peripheral neuropathy. Normal gait and balance.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal neuro exam argues against B12 deficiency which causes subacute combined degeneration (posterior columns and corticospinal tracts)

physicalExam.finding: Normal tone. Brown stool, guaiac negative.
physicalExam.reasoning: Negative guaiac argues against significant ongoing GI blood loss, supporting menorrhagia as the source

labs.name: CBC with Differential
labs.result: WBC 6.8, Hgb 9.2 (LOW), Hct 28%, MCV 72 (LOW), MCH 24 (LOW), MCHC 31, RDW 18% (HIGH), Plt 320
labs.reasoning: Microcytic anemia (low MCV). High RDW indicates variation in cell size (anisocytosis) - classic for iron deficiency. Platelet count mildly elevated (reactive).

labs.name: Reticulocyte Count
labs.reasoning: Low reticulocyte index indicates marrow not responding appropriately - production problem (iron deficiency), not hemolysis or bleeding compensation.

labs.name: Serum Iron
labs.reasoning: Low serum iron indicates depleted iron. Part of iron studies panel.

labs.name: TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity)
labs.reasoning: HIGH TIBC is key finding - body is upregulating transferrin to try to absorb more iron. Low in ACD, high in iron deficiency.

labs.name: Ferritin
labs.reasoning: Low ferritin is most SENSITIVE marker for iron deficiency - reflects iron stores. <12 is essentially diagnostic.

labs.name: Transferrin Saturation
labs.reasoning: Iron saturation = serum iron/TIBC. <20% indicates iron deficiency.

labs.name: Peripheral Blood Smear
labs.result: Microcytic, hypochromic red cells. Anisocytosis and poikilocytosis. Target cells and pencil cells present.
labs.reasoning: Classic iron deficiency smear: microcytic, hypochromic, variable size (anisocytosis), pencil/cigar cells

labs.name: B12 and Folate
labs.reasoning: Normal - rules out concurrent B12 or folate deficiency. Would check if MCV elevated or mixed picture.

imaging.name: Pelvic Ultrasound
imaging.result: Consider to evaluate for uterine fibroids or other structural causes of menorrhagia
imaging.reasoning: May identify fibroids or adenomyosis as cause of heavy bleeding. Would guide gynecologic management.

treatments.name: Oral Iron Supplementation
treatments.result: Ferrous sulfate 325mg (65mg elemental iron) TID on empty stomach with vitamin C
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment. Take on empty stomach for absorption. Vitamin C enhances absorption. Avoid with calcium, antacids, or tea.

treatments.name: Iron supplementation counseling
treatments.result: Warn about GI side effects (constipation, nausea, dark stools). May take 3-6 months to replete stores.
treatments.reasoning: Common side effects limit compliance. Dark stools are expected (iron). Takes time to replete - check Hgb in 4-8 weeks.

treatments.name: Dietary counseling
treatments.result: Increase iron-rich foods: red meat (if willing), fortified cereals, legumes, leafy greens. Avoid coffee/tea with meals.
treatments.reasoning: Dietary changes can help but unlikely to fully replete stores alone. Vegetarian sources have non-heme iron (less bioavailable).

treatments.name: Address underlying blood loss
treatments.result: Gynecology referral to address menorrhagia. Options include hormonal management, IUD, or procedural intervention.
treatments.reasoning: MUST address the source of blood loss or iron deficiency will recur. Heavy menstrual bleeding should be evaluated and treated.

consults.name: Gynecology Referral
consults.result: Refer for evaluation and management of menorrhagia
consults.reasoning: Need to address heavy menstrual bleeding to prevent recurrence of iron deficiency.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with follow-up
disposition.result: Follow up in 4-8 weeks to recheck Hgb and reticulocyte count
disposition.reasoning: Hgb should increase ~1g/dL per week with adequate iron replacement. Reticulocyte count rises first (within days).

actualDiagnosis.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia Secondary to Menorrhagia
keyFindings: Symptoms: Fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, pica (ice craving - pagophagia)
History of menorrhagia: Changing super tampon q1-2h, clots, 7-day periods
Physical exam: Pallor, koilonychia (spoon nails), flow murmur
CBC: Microcytic anemia (MCV 72), Hgb 9.2, high RDW (anisocytosis)
Iron studies: Low ferritin (8), low iron (28), HIGH TIBC (450), low saturation (6%)
Smear: Microcytic, hypochromic RBCs, pencil cells
Clear source identified: Heavy menstrual bleeding
teachingPoints: Approach to anemia: First classify by MCV - Microcytic (<80), Normocytic (80-100), Macrocytic (>100)
Microcytic anemia differential: Iron deficiency, Thalassemia, Anemia of chronic disease (can be), Sideroblastic, Lead poisoning (TAILS mnemonic)
Iron deficiency pattern: Low ferritin, Low iron, HIGH TIBC, Low transferrin saturation, High RDW
Anemia of chronic disease pattern: Low iron, LOW TIBC, Normal/high ferritin. Both iron and TIBC are LOW.
Thalassemia: Low MCV but normal iron studies. Very low MCV relative to Hgb. RDW is normal (uniform microcytosis).
Ferritin is the most SENSITIVE test for iron deficiency. Ferritin <12 is essentially diagnostic. But ferritin is acute phase reactant - can be falsely elevated with inflammation.
ALWAYS find the source of iron deficiency: Premenopausal women = menstrual loss. Men/postmenopausal = GI loss (scope to rule out cancer!)
Pica/Pagophagia (ice craving) is specific for iron deficiency - ask about it!
Koilonychia (spoon nails) is specific for iron deficiency - examine the nails!
Oral iron: Takes 3-6 months to replete stores. Hgb should rise ~1g/dL per week. GI side effects common. Vitamin C enhances absorption.
If oral iron fails: Consider IV iron (better tolerated, faster repletion). Also consider celiac disease causing malabsorption.

guidelineReferences.name: ASH Guidelines on Iron Deficiency Anemia 2020
guidelineReferences.citation: American Society of Hematology. Blood Adv. 2020;4(7):1384-1394
keyPoints: Ferritin <30 ng/mL diagnostic for iron deficiency
Oral iron first-line; IV iron if intolerant or malabsorption
Always identify and treat underlying cause
GI evaluation in men and postmenopausal women
Hgb rise of 1-2 g/dL expected in 4 weeks

guidelineReferences.name: AGA Guidelines for GI Evaluation of Iron Deficiency Anemia
keyPoints: Bidirectional endoscopy for men and postmenopausal women
Rule out GI malignancy as source
Consider celiac disease testing
Repeat endoscopy if initial workup negative and anemia persists
Capsule endoscopy for obscure GI bleeding

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the MCV was elevated (macrocytic)?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same fatigue but MCV 112, with neurological symptoms

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling exhausted for months
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been exhausted for months and my hands and feet are numb and tingly

mustNotMiss: B12 deficiency (can cause irreversible neuro damage)

feedback.correct: Macrocytic anemia with neurological symptoms = B12 deficiency until proven otherwise. Neuro findings (paresthesias, ataxia, cognitive changes) can be IRREVERSIBLE if not treated. Start B12 before waiting for results if suspected.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of macrocytic pattern. What's the critical complication?
feedback.incorrect: Macrocytic anemia (MCV >100): think B12, folate, alcohol, hypothyroidism, MDS, liver disease. Neurological symptoms = B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration). Start treatment promptly - neuro damage can be irreversible!

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Microcytic = iron deficiency, thalassemia. Macrocytic = B12, folate, alcohol, hypothyroid. B12 deficiency has neuro findings that folate does NOT have (and folate can mask B12 deficiency hematologically while neuro damage progresses).

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was a 65-year-old man with iron deficiency?
whatIfScenarios.description: Iron deficiency anemia in elderly male with no obvious bleeding source

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling exhausted for months
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm a 65-year-old man feeling exhausted. Labs show iron deficiency anemia.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: GI malignancy causing occult blood loss

feedback.correct: Iron deficiency in men/postmenopausal women = GI malignancy until proven otherwise. MUST scope both ends (EGD + colonoscopy). Colon cancer is the diagnosis you cannot miss.
feedback.partial: Good concern for GI source. What workup is required?
feedback.incorrect: Iron deficiency anemia in men or postmenopausal women = OCCULT GI BLOOD LOSS. Must rule out GI malignancy with EGD AND colonoscopy. Don't just treat the anemia - find the cancer!

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Premenopausal women = menstrual loss is presumed cause (but scope if not responding to iron or >50yo). Men/postmenopausal = ALWAYS scope - assume cancer until proven otherwise.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were signs of hemolysis?
whatIfScenarios.description: Anemia with jaundice, dark urine, elevated LDH and bilirubin, low haptoglobin

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling exhausted for months
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm exhausted, my eyes look yellow, and my urine has been dark brown

feedback.correct: Hemolysis labs: elevated LDH, low haptoglobin (consumed), elevated indirect bilirubin, high reticulocyte count. Check smear for schistocytes (MAHA) vs spherocytes (AIHA/HS). Direct Coombs for autoimmune hemolysis.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of hemolysis. What tests differentiate the causes?
feedback.incorrect: Hemolysis markers: High LDH, LOW haptoglobin (most specific), high indirect bili, high reticulocytes. Peripheral smear is KEY: schistocytes = microangiopathic (TTP/HUS/DIC), spherocytes = autoimmune or hereditary spherocytosis. Coombs test differentiates.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Decreased production (iron def, B12) = low reticulocytes. Increased destruction (hemolysis) = high reticulocytes. Hemolysis pattern: high LDH, low haptoglobin, high indirect bili. Smear morphology tells you the cause.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

male-24-voc.name: Timothy Williams
male-24-voc.chiefComplaint: I'm having a pain crisis - my back and legs are killing me
male-24-voc.variantDescription: Vaso-occlusive crisis (VOC) in sickle cell disease

missedConsequences.Acute Chest Syndrome: Leading cause of death in SCD. New infiltrate + respiratory symptoms = emergency.
missedConsequences.Aplastic Crisis: Parvovirus B19 shuts down RBC production. Severe anemia without reticulocytes.

commonPitfalls: Undertreating pain - patients know their pain, believe them
Missing acute chest syndrome - get CXR even if lungs clear
Giving O2 when not hypoxic (suppresses erythropoiesis)
Missing infection as trigger

mcqs.question: Which finding is MOST concerning for acute chest syndrome?

options.explanation: Severe pain is expected in VOC.

options.text: New pulmonary infiltrate with hypoxia and chest pain
options.explanation: ACS = new infiltrate + respiratory symptoms. Leading cause of death in SCD.
Baseline Hgb in SCD is often 7-9.
Low fever can occur with VOC itself.

mcqs.question: What is the BEST initial pain management for severe VOC?

options.explanation: Undertreating is harmful. Severe VOC needs IV opioids.

options.text: IV opioids with PCA if available
options.explanation: Aggressive IV opioid therapy is standard. Believe the patient's pain.
Stigmatizing. VOC requires IV treatment.
NSAIDs are adjunct only, not monotherapy.

allDiagnoses.name: Vaso-occlusive Crisis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic: severe pain in back/legs, known SCD, triggered by cold/dehydration.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Chest Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: New infiltrate + resp symptoms. Can develop during VOC. Leading cause of death.

allDiagnoses.name: Aplastic Crisis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Parvovirus B19. Severe anemia, low reticulocytes.

allDiagnoses.name: Osteomyelitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Salmonella common in SCD. Focal bone pain + fever.

male-24-voc.oldcartsNarrative: "I have sickle cell and I'm having a crisis. The pain started last night after I got caught in the cold rain. It's in my lower back, both legs, and my chest. It's a 10 out of 10 - you have to help me. I've been through this before and I know what works - I usually need IV dilaudid. I tried drinking fluids and taking my oxycodone at home but it's not touching it. I feel short of breath too. I'm on hydroxyurea and folic acid. My last crisis was 3 months ago."

historyQuestions.text: When did this pain start?
historyQuestions.answer: It started last night, around 10pm. I got caught in cold rain earlier and started feeling the pain a few hours later. It's been getting worse since then.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline helps identify trigger and assess duration of crisis

historyQuestions.text: Where is the pain?
historyQuestions.answer: My lower back, both thighs and calves, and my chest. These are my usual spots when I have a crisis.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Typical VOC locations: back, extremities, chest. Patients often have consistent patterns.

historyQuestions.text: What triggered this?
historyQuestions.answer: I got caught in cold rain yesterday and got chilled. Cold always triggers my crises.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cold, dehydration, infection, stress are common triggers.

historyQuestions.text: Any trouble breathing?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I feel a little short of breath and my chest hurts when I breathe deep.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Respiratory symptoms + chest pain = must rule out ACS.

historyQuestions.text: What's your baseline hemoglobin?
historyQuestions.answer: Usually around 8 when I'm not in crisis. Sometimes drops to 7 during a bad one.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Knowing baseline helps interpret current Hgb.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you on?
historyQuestions.answer: Hydroxyurea, folic acid, and oxycodone for breakthrough pain at home.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hydroxyurea reduces crisis frequency. Important for management.

historyQuestions.text: What usually helps your pain?
historyQuestions.answer: IV dilaudid is what works for me. Morphine makes me itch. I usually need PCA.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patients know what works. Believe them and use their regimen.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any fevers or felt sick?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt a little warm but no real high fevers. No sore throat or cough before this started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever can indicate infection triggering crisis or complicating ACS

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had acute chest syndrome before?
historyQuestions.answer: Once, about 2 years ago. They had to give me a blood transfusion that time. It was scary.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior ACS increases risk for recurrence; affects threshold for transfusion

historyQuestions.text: When was your last blood transfusion?
historyQuestions.answer: About 2 years ago during that chest syndrome episode. I try to avoid them because of iron overload.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Transfusion history affects antibody status and iron overload risk

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: Morphine makes me super itchy - I can't tolerate it. No other allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for pain management - morphine reaction noted

historyQuestions.text: What did you try at home?
historyQuestions.answer: I drank a lot of water, took my oxycodone - 3 doses - and used a heating pad. Nothing touched it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Documents failed home management and oral opioid use

historyQuestions.text: How often do you have crises?
historyQuestions.answer: Used to be every month or two before hydroxyurea. Now maybe 2-3 times a year. This is my second one this year.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hydroxyurea compliance and crisis frequency affects long-term management

historyQuestions.text: How many times have you been hospitalized for crises?
historyQuestions.answer: Too many to count. Maybe 20 times over my life. I know this drill.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Frequent hospitalizations indicate severe phenotype

historyQuestions.text: Who is your hematologist?
historyQuestions.answer: Dr. Williams at the sickle cell center. She has all my records.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hematologist can provide baseline information and management plan

historyQuestions.text: Who's here with you?
historyQuestions.answer: My girlfriend drove me. She's in the waiting room.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Support system for admission and discharge planning

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 138/85, HR 105, RR 20, Temp 100.8°F, SpO2 94% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia, low-grade fever, borderline hypoxia. SpO2 94% concerning - get CXR.

physicalExam.finding: Young man in obvious distress, writhing in pain, diaphoretic. Scleral icterus present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe pain is real. Icterus from chronic hemolysis is baseline in SCD.

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds at right base. Dullness to percussion RLL. Mild crackles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Abnormal lung exam with hypoxia = high concern for ACS. Must get CXR.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mild LUQ tenderness. No splenomegaly (likely auto-infarcted). No hepatomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: Most adults with SCD have auto-infarcted spleens. Splenomegaly would be concerning.

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse tenderness in bilateral thighs and lower back. No focal bone tenderness. No swelling or erythema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diffuse tenderness is typical of VOC. Focal tenderness might suggest osteomyelitis.

labs.name: CBC with Reticulocyte Count
labs.reasoning: Hgb slightly below baseline. Elevated retic = marrow responding. Low retic would suggest aplastic crisis.

labs.name: Reticulocyte Count
labs.reasoning: Elevated reticulocytes = marrow responding. If LOW, suspect parvovirus aplastic crisis.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Check renal function - SCD can cause nephropathy.

labs.name: LDH, Bilirubin
labs.reasoning: Elevated from chronic hemolysis - baseline in SCD.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.result: Type O positive. Antibody screen positive (multiple alloantibodies).
labs.reasoning: SCD patients often have alloantibodies from transfusions. May need phenotypically matched blood.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: New right lower lobe infiltrate. Small right pleural effusion.
imaging.reasoning: New infiltrate + symptoms = ACUTE CHEST SYNDROME. This changes management significantly.

treatments.name: IV Fluids
treatments.reasoning: Hydration is key but avoid overhydration which can worsen ACS.

treatments.name: IV Opioids
treatments.result: Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) 1-2mg IV q15-20min until controlled, then PCA
treatments.reasoning: Aggressive pain control is standard. Use patient's known effective regimen.

treatments.name: Supplemental Oxygen
treatments.result: O2 to maintain SpO2 >95% (has hypoxia, so appropriate)
treatments.reasoning: Give O2 only if hypoxic. This patient is hypoxic so O2 is appropriate.

treatments.name: Antibiotics for ACS
treatments.reasoning: ACS may have infectious component. Cover typical and atypical pathogens.

treatments.name: Simple vs Exchange Transfusion
treatments.result: Simple transfusion for moderate ACS. Exchange transfusion if severe.
treatments.reasoning: Transfusion improves oxygen carrying capacity. Exchange for severe ACS (PaO2 <60, rapid progression).

treatments.name: Incentive Spirometry
treatments.reasoning: Prevents atelectasis and worsening of ACS. Critical preventive measure.

consults.name: Hematology
consults.result: Hematology consulted for SCD management and transfusion decisions
consults.reasoning: Hematology guides transfusion, exchange decisions, and chronic management.

disposition.name: Admit to hospital
disposition.result: Admit for VOC + Acute Chest Syndrome
disposition.reasoning: ACS requires inpatient management with close monitoring.

actualDiagnosis.name: Vaso-occlusive Crisis complicated by Acute Chest Syndrome
keyFindings: Known SCD with typical VOC: severe pain in back, legs, chest
Trigger identified: cold exposure
Hypoxia (SpO2 94%) with respiratory symptoms
New RLL infiltrate on CXR = Acute Chest Syndrome
Low-grade fever, tachycardia
Elevated reticulocyte count (appropriate marrow response)
teachingPoints: VOC is most common reason for SCD hospitalization. Triggers: cold, dehydration, infection, stress, hypoxia.
ACUTE CHEST SYNDROME: New infiltrate + fever/cough/chest pain/hypoxia. Leading cause of death in SCD.
ACS can develop DURING hospitalization for VOC. Monitor closely and get CXR if any respiratory symptoms.
PAIN MANAGEMENT: Believe the patient. Aggressive IV opioids, preferably PCA. Patients know what works for them.
Avoid O2 unless hypoxic - can suppress erythropoietin and worsen anemia.
Incentive spirometry q2h prevents ACS. It's one of the most important interventions.
Transfusion: Simple for moderate ACS. Exchange transfusion for severe ACS (PaO2 <60, multi-lobe, rapid progression).
Aplastic crisis: Parvovirus B19 causes reticulocyte count to DROP. Suspect if anemia with low retics.
Splenic sequestration: Rapid pooling in spleen. More common in children. LUQ pain, splenomegaly, dropping Hgb.
Hydroxyurea reduces crisis frequency by increasing HbF. Check compliance.

guidelineReferences.name: ASH 2020 Guidelines for Sickle Cell Disease
keyPoints: Aggressive IV opioids for VOC pain - PCA preferred, believe the patient
Incentive spirometry q2h is critical for ACS prevention
Simple transfusion for moderate ACS; exchange for severe (PaO2 <60, multi-lobe)
Hydroxyurea for all adults with SCA to reduce crisis frequency
Target HbS <30% for stroke and exchange transfusion

guidelineReferences.name: NIH Evidence-Based Management of Sickle Cell Disease 2014
guidelineReferences.citation: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute
keyPoints: ACS defined as: new pulmonary infiltrate + fever/respiratory symptoms
ACS triggers: fat embolism, infection, in situ sickling
Supplemental O2 only if SpO2 <92% (high O2 may suppress erythropoiesis)
IV fluids at maintenance rate (avoid over-hydration which worsens ACS)
Early empiric antibiotics covering atypicals for ACS

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Otherwise healthy and active. This illness is unexpected and concerning.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Young adult with supportive family and friends. Active social life.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had sudden neurological changes?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same crisis but with acute left-sided weakness and slurred speech

chiefComplaint.original: My whole body is hurting like my usual crisis
chiefComplaint.modified: My body hurts and suddenly I can't move my left side and my speech is slurred

feedback.correct: SCD stroke is a EMERGENCY. CT head immediately (to rule out hemorrhage), then exchange transfusion is treatment of choice. Target HbS <30%. tPA rarely used in SCD stroke.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of stroke. What's the specific treatment for SCD stroke?
feedback.incorrect: SCD patients have 300x increased stroke risk. EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION is treatment of choice - goal HbS <30%. CT head first to rule out hemorrhage. tPA controversial/rarely used. Transcranial doppler screening can identify high-risk patients.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Simple VOC = pain management, hydration. Stroke = exchange transfusion emergency. SCD strokes are often recurrent without chronic transfusion therapy.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the reticulocyte count was low?
whatIfScenarios.description: Sickle cell patient with severe anemia but reticulocyte count only 0.5%

chiefComplaint.original: My whole body is hurting like my usual crisis
chiefComplaint.modified: I feel extremely weak and tired, more than usual. I had a cold last week with a rash.

feedback.correct: Low reticulocytes in SCD = APLASTIC CRISIS. Parvovirus B19 infects RBC precursors, stopping production. Needs transfusion until marrow recovers (7-10 days). Isolate from pregnant women!
feedback.partial: Good recognition of aplastic crisis. What's the causative agent?
feedback.incorrect: Normally SCD patients have HIGH reticulocytes (marrow compensating for hemolysis). LOW retics = bone marrow failure = APLASTIC CRISIS. Usually Parvovirus B19. Needs transfusion support until recovery. Self-limited but dangerous.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Hemolytic crisis = high retics (marrow working hard). Aplastic crisis = low retics (marrow shut down). The low retics are the key distinguishing feature.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had a painful erection?
whatIfScenarios.description: Male SCD patient with painful erection lasting 5 hours

chiefComplaint.original: My whole body is hurting like my usual crisis
chiefComplaint.modified: I have a painful erection that won't go away - it's been 5 hours

feedback.correct: Priapism >4 hours is urologic emergency. Ischemic type needs corporal aspiration + phenylephrine injection. If >24-48 hours, may need surgical shunt. Delays cause erectile dysfunction.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of priapism emergency. What's the initial treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Priapism = erection >4 hours. In SCD, usually ischemic (low-flow) type. EMERGENCY - corporal blood gas shows ischemia. Treatment: aspiration of blood from corpora + intracavernosal phenylephrine. Delays >24h often cause permanent ED.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Stuttering priapism (<4 hours, resolves) = common in SCD, manage outpatient. Major priapism (>4 hours) = emergency, aspiration + phenylephrine, urology consult.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Clinical Guideline: GI Evaluation of Iron Deficiency Anemia
guidelineReferences.citation: Ko CW, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2020;115:1007-1021
keyPoints: Bidirectional endoscopy recommended in most adults with IDA
Colonoscopy required in patients >45 to rule out malignancy
Celiac serologies should be checked
Consider capsule endoscopy if upper and lower GI normal

adult-52-ida.name: J. Smith
adult-52-ida.chiefComplaint: I've been feeling really tired and short of breath for a few months
adult-52-ida.variantDescription: Iron deficiency anemia requiring GI workup for occult malignancy

missedConsequences.Occult GI Bleeding: Iron deficiency treated with iron supplements alone without investigating cause. Patient presents 8 months later with Stage IV colon cancer that was Stage I when anemia first discovered.
missedConsequences.Iron Deficiency Anemia: Anemia attributed to 'heavy periods' without treatment. Patient becomes severely symptomatic with Hgb 5, requires emergent transfusion.
missedConsequences.CHF Exacerbation: If CHF were present and missed, patient could develop flash pulmonary edema.

commonPitfalls: Attributing iron deficiency to diet or menses without GI evaluation in patients >50 - COLON CANCER must be ruled out!
Treating anemia with iron alone without finding the source of iron loss
Missing pica (ice craving) as a pathognomonic symptom of iron deficiency - always ask!
Not recognizing the MCV pattern: microcytic anemia + low ferritin = iron deficiency
Forgetting that iron deficiency can cause heart failure symptoms in someone with prior cardiac history
Not examining for signs of anemia: pallor, glossitis, koilonychia (spoon nails)

mcqs.question: The patient craves ice constantly. What is the clinical significance of this symptom?

options.text: It's just a behavioral quirk with no clinical significance
options.explanation: Pica has important diagnostic significance and shouldn't be dismissed.

options.text: Pagophagia (ice craving) is highly specific for iron deficiency anemia
options.explanation: Pica, especially pagophagia (ice craving), is PATHOGNOMONIC for iron deficiency. The mechanism is unclear but it resolves with iron replacement. Always ask about unusual cravings!

options.text: It suggests the patient is dehydrated
options.explanation: Dehydration doesn't cause pica.
Pica can be part of eating disorders, but in this context it strongly suggests iron deficiency.

mcqs.question: This 52-year-old has iron deficiency anemia. She attributes it to her vegetarian diet and heavy periods. What's the MOST important next step?

options.text: Start iron supplements and recheck CBC in 3 months
options.explanation: WRONG! You cannot just treat iron deficiency in a patient >50 without evaluating for GI source.

options.text: Refer to gynecology for menorrhagia evaluation
options.explanation: While menorrhagia should be addressed, this doesn't rule out GI malignancy which is the bigger concern.

options.text: Colonoscopy to rule out occult GI malignancy (colon cancer)
options.explanation: In ANY patient over 50 with iron deficiency anemia, you MUST rule out colon cancer. Don't be reassured by vegetarian diet or heavy menses - these can coexist with malignancy.
Diet alone won't correct established iron deficiency, and this completely misses the need to rule out GI malignancy.

allDiagnoses.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, pica (ice chewing), vegetarian diet, heavy menses. Exam findings of pallor and glossitis support this.

allDiagnoses.name: Anemia of Chronic Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider given prior MI. However, no active inflammatory condition identified.

allDiagnoses.name: Occult GI Bleeding
allDiagnoses.rationale: CRITICAL in anyone with iron deficiency anemia, especially over age 50. Must rule out colon cancer. GI workup required.

allDiagnoses.name: CHF Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: History of prior MI, stopped diuretic. Must evaluate for heart failure as cause of dyspnea.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypothyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Some symptoms overlap (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, sluggish thinking). Should check TSH.

allDiagnoses.name: COPD
allDiagnoses.rationale: Former smoker but quit 5 years ago. No chronic cough, no wheezing. Dyspnea pattern doesn't fit.

allDiagnoses.name: Deconditioning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sedentary lifestyle could contribute but doesn't explain pallor, pica, or lab abnormalities.

adult-52-ida.oldcartsNarrative: "For the past 3-4 months I've been feeling exhausted all the time. It doesn't matter how much I sleep, I wake up tired. And I get short of breath way easier than I used to - just walking up a flight of stairs or across the parking lot leaves me winded. Sometimes my heart feels like it's racing after I exert myself. I had a mild heart attack about 5 years ago and I'm worried it might be my heart again. I've been vegetarian for years and don't eat much red meat. Here's the strange thing - I've been craving ice constantly. I just want to chew on ice all day. I know that's weird. My periods have always been heavy - usually last about a week with lots of pad changes. Oh, and I've been feeling cold all the time lately and gained about 5 pounds. I stopped taking my water pill about a month ago because it made me pee too much at night."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first start noticing these symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: It's been going on for about 3-4 months. It started gradually - at first I thought I was just not sleeping well, but it's been getting progressively worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset over months is typical for iron deficiency anemia developing from chronic blood loss

historyQuestions.text: Describe the fatigue - is it constant or does it come and go?
historyQuestions.answer: It's constant. I wake up tired, I'm tired all day, I go to bed tired. Doesn't matter how much sleep I get. It's been getting worse over months.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constant fatigue not relieved by rest suggests systemic cause (anemia, thyroid) rather than sleep disorder or deconditioning.

historyQuestions.text: When do you get short of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: With activity - stairs, walking across parking lots, carrying groceries. Things that never used to bother me. It goes away when I rest.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Exertional dyspnea relieved by rest seen in both anemia (decreased O2 carrying capacity) and CHF (cardiac limitation).

historyQuestions.text: Do you need extra pillows to sleep? Do you wake up gasping at night?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I sleep flat with one pillow. No waking up short of breath at night.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No orthopnea or PND argues against CHF exacerbation despite stopping diuretic.

historyQuestions.text: Any swelling in your legs or feet?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my legs look normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No peripheral edema further argues against CHF.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned craving ice - tell me more about that.
historyQuestions.answer: I can't stop chewing ice. I go through trays of ice cubes. I've been doing it for a couple months. I know it's strange but I just crave it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PICA (craving non-nutritive substances, especially ice/pagophagia) is HIGHLY SPECIFIC for iron deficiency anemia. This is almost diagnostic.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your diet.
historyQuestions.answer: I've been vegetarian for about 10 years. I don't eat red meat at all. I try to eat beans and greens but I know I probably don't get enough iron.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vegetarian diet with limited heme iron intake is a risk factor for iron deficiency.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your menstrual periods.
historyQuestions.answer: They've always been heavy. Usually last about 7 days. I go through a lot of pads, sometimes have to change every couple hours on heavy days. No clots though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy menstrual bleeding is the most common cause of iron deficiency in premenopausal women.

historyQuestions.text: Any blood in your stool? Black or tarry stools? Vomiting blood?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my stools look normal. No blood that I've seen.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No overt GI bleeding symptoms, BUT occult bleeding can still occur. Must do GI workup for iron deficiency in patients over 50.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned feeling cold - any other symptoms like that?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I'm always cold when others are comfortable. I've also noticed I feel kind of sluggish mentally, like my thinking is slower. And I gained about 5 pounds.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cold intolerance, weight gain, cognitive slowing could suggest hypothyroidism. Should check TSH.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your heart attack.
historyQuestions.answer: It was 5 years ago, pretty mild they said. I didn't get a stent. I've been on metoprolol and lisinopril since then. No problems until now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior MI is risk factor for CHF. Important history but current presentation doesn't fit CHF pattern.

historyQuestions.text: Why did you stop the water pill?
historyQuestions.answer: It made me pee so much at night, I couldn't sleep. So I just stopped it about a month ago. Was that bad?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Stopping furosemide could precipitate CHF, but patient denies orthopnea, PND, edema. Anemia more likely cause of symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

diet_iron_intake.answer: I don't eat much red meat anymore. Mostly chicken, some fish. Trying to eat healthier after my heart attack.
diet_iron_intake.relevance: Low red meat intake reduces dietary iron - important consideration in iron deficiency, especially in older adults.

diet_eating_patterns.answer: Since my wife passed, I don't cook much. A lot of sandwiches, cereal, canned soup. Not much variety.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: Widower diet - poor nutritional variety common after spouse loss; contributes to nutritional deficiencies including iron.

social_living_situation.answer: Live alone now. My wife passed 2 years ago. Kids check in but they're busy with their own families.
social_living_situation.relevance: Social isolation after spousal loss affects nutrition, healthcare utilization, and overall health - important social determinant.

occupation_past_exposures.answer: Retired electrician. 35 years in the trade. Sometimes worked around lead in old buildings.
occupation_past_exposures.relevance: Historical lead exposure can cause anemia; less likely primary cause here but relevant occupational history.

social_nsaid_use.answer: I take ibuprofen (Advil) for my arthritis. Probably 4-6 pills a day. Helps me get around.
social_nsaid_use.relevance: Daily NSAID use is major risk factor for occult GI bleeding - likely contributor to iron deficiency in this patient.

social_alcohol_use.answer: I have a few drinks. More than I used to since Martha passed. Maybe 3-4 beers most nights.
social_alcohol_use.relevance: Increased alcohol use can cause GI irritation, interfere with nutrient absorption, and indicates possible depression/grief.

social_healthcare_access.answer: I don't like going to doctors. Haven't had my colonoscopy they keep telling me to get.
social_healthcare_access.relevance: Avoiding screening colonoscopy means possible occult GI malignancy contributing to iron deficiency - needs workup.

housing_food_security.answer: Money's tight on fixed income. I try to stretch things. Sometimes skip meals.
housing_food_security.relevance: Food insecurity affects nutritional intake; fixed income may limit access to iron-rich foods.

physicalExam.finding: BP 122/74, HR 94 (mildly elevated), RR 16, Temp 98.2°F, SpO2 98% on RA.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild tachycardia can be compensatory response to anemia. Normal oxygen saturation.

physicalExam.finding: Fatigued-appearing but alert. Speaks in full sentences. Pale complexion. No acute distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor is key finding for anemia. Fatigue visible but not severely ill-appearing.

physicalExam.finding: Conjunctival pallor present bilaterally. Pale lower palpebral conjunctivae.
physicalExam.reasoning: CONJUNCTIVAL PALLOR is sensitive bedside test for anemia, especially when Hgb <9.

physicalExam.finding: Glossitis present - tongue appears smooth, red, with loss of papillae. Mild angular cheilitis at corners of mouth.
physicalExam.reasoning: GLOSSITIS (smooth, beefy red tongue) and angular cheilitis are classic iron deficiency findings.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rhythm, tachycardic. Soft systolic flow murmur at LLSB (grade 1-2/6). No S3 or S4 gallop. No heaves or thrills.
physicalExam.reasoning: FLOW MURMUR is common with anemia - increased blood flow velocity across normal valves. No S3 argues against CHF.

physicalExam.finding: JVP not elevated (normal). No hepatojugular reflux.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal JVP strongly argues against CHF despite stopping diuretic.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clear lungs rule out pulmonary edema (CHF) and suggest no active COPD exacerbation.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. No hepatomegaly. Spleen not palpable. No masses.
physicalExam.reasoning: No splenomegaly (would suggest hemolytic anemia or other causes). No masses.

physicalExam.finding: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness. Nails slightly concave (koilonychia beginning).
physicalExam.reasoning: No edema rules out CHF. Koilonychia (spoon nails) is classic but late finding of iron deficiency.

physicalExam.finding: Thyroid normal size, no nodules, no tenderness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal thyroid exam but doesn't rule out hypothyroidism. Need TSH.

physicalExam.finding: Normal tone. No masses. Stool guaiac POSITIVE (occult blood detected).
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Positive guaiac indicates occult GI bleeding. Requires colonoscopy, especially at age 52.

labs.name: CBC with indices
labs.result: WBC 6.5, Hgb 8.2 g/dL (LOW), Hct 25%, MCV 72 fL (LOW - microcytic), RDW 18% (HIGH), Plt 420 (HIGH)
labs.reasoning: MICROCYTIC ANEMIA (low MCV) with elevated RDW and reactive thrombocytosis - classic pattern for iron deficiency anemia.

labs.name: Iron studies (Fe, TIBC, ferritin)
labs.result: Serum iron: 25 μg/dL (LOW), TIBC: 450 μg/dL (HIGH), Ferritin: 8 ng/mL (LOW), Transferrin saturation: 6% (LOW)
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC for iron deficiency: low iron, low ferritin, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation. This is the classic pattern.

labs.name: Reticulocyte count
labs.reasoning: Low reticulocyte response indicates marrow isn't producing enough RBCs despite anemia - consistent with iron deficiency.

labs.name: TSH
labs.reasoning: Normal TSH rules out hypothyroidism as cause of fatigue symptoms.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Normal renal function rules out anemia of chronic kidney disease.

labs.name: BNP
labs.reasoning: Normal BNP argues strongly against CHF as cause of dyspnea.

labs.name: Vitamin B12 and Folate
labs.reasoning: Rules out B12/folate deficiency which cause macrocytic anemia. This patient has microcytic anemia so less likely but reasonable to check.

labs.name: Peripheral blood smear
labs.result: Microcytic, hypochromic RBCs. Pencil cells (elliptocytes). Anisocytosis. No target cells.
labs.reasoning: Confirms iron deficiency pattern. Pencil cells are characteristic. Target cells would suggest thalassemia.

labs.name: Hemoglobin electrophoresis
labs.reasoning: Reasonable to check if considering thalassemia trait as cause of microcytosis, but ferritin already confirms iron deficiency.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: Normal AST, ALT, Alk Phos, Bilirubin.
labs.reasoning: Helps rule out liver disease. Normal indirect bilirubin argues against hemolysis.

labs.name: LDH and Haptoglobin
labs.reasoning: Rules out hemolytic anemia. Not the primary concern here given iron deficiency pattern.

labs.name: Celiac Disease Panel (tTG-IgA)
labs.reasoning: Celiac can cause iron deficiency from malabsorption. Reasonable to check if no obvious GI blood loss source found.

labs.name: Erythropoietin level
labs.result: Not indicated - iron deficiency already established.
labs.reasoning: EPO levels not helpful when cause of anemia is clear. Would be elevated appropriately in iron deficiency.

labs.name: Bone marrow biopsy
labs.reasoning: Bone marrow is gold standard for iron stores but almost never needed. Ferritin is sufficient for diagnosis.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Normal heart size. Lungs clear. No pulmonary edema.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms no CHF. Reasonable given cardiac history and dyspnea.

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: EF 50% (mildly reduced but stable from prior). No wall motion abnormalities. No significant valvular disease.
imaging.reasoning: Stable from prior. Not urgent unless CHF suspected. Dyspnea explained by anemia.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Not first-line - colonoscopy/EGD preferred for GI bleeding workup.
imaging.reasoning: Endoscopy is preferred for occult GI bleeding evaluation. CT may miss mucosal lesions.

imaging.name: Capsule endoscopy
imaging.result: Consider if EGD and colonoscopy negative - evaluates small bowel.
imaging.reasoning: Reserved for obscure GI bleeding when upper and lower endoscopy negative.

procedures.name: Colonoscopy
procedures.result: REQUIRED - patient is over 50 with iron deficiency anemia and positive stool guaiac. Must rule out colon cancer.
procedures.reasoning: MANDATORY: Iron deficiency anemia in ANYONE over 50 requires GI workup to rule out malignancy. Do not skip this!

procedures.name: Upper endoscopy (EGD)
procedures.result: Should be performed to evaluate upper GI source of blood loss.
procedures.reasoning: Bidirectional endoscopy (both EGD and colonoscopy) recommended for IDA in older adults. Can find gastric/duodenal sources.

procedures.name: Stool guaiac / FIT
procedures.reasoning: Already positive on exam. Confirms occult GI bleeding.

treatments.name: Oral iron (ferrous sulfate 325mg daily)
treatments.result: Ferrous sulfate 325mg PO daily started. Take on empty stomach with vitamin C. Expect constipation, dark stools.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment for iron deficiency. Takes 2-3 months to replenish stores. Reticulocyte count should rise in 1-2 weeks.

treatments.name: IV iron infusion
treatments.result: Consider if oral iron not tolerated or not absorbing.
treatments.reasoning: Reserved for oral iron intolerance, malabsorption, or need for rapid repletion. More expensive, requires infusion.

treatments.name: Dietary iron counseling
treatments.result: Counseled on iron-rich foods, importance of heme iron, vitamin C to enhance absorption, avoid tea/coffee with iron.
treatments.reasoning: Dietary modification alone rarely corrects iron deficiency but helps maintain stores.

treatments.name: Blood transfusion
treatments.result: Not indicated currently - patient is symptomatic but stable with Hgb 8.2.
treatments.reasoning: Transfusion reserved for severe anemia (typically Hgb <7) or hemodynamic instability. Oral iron is appropriate here.

treatments.name: Erythropoietin (EPO) injection
treatments.result: NOT indicated - patient does not have chronic kidney disease.
treatments.reasoning: EPO used for anemia of CKD, not iron deficiency. Iron deficiency must be corrected first anyway.

treatments.name: Vitamin B12 injection
treatments.result: NOT indicated - this is iron deficiency, not B12 deficiency.
treatments.reasoning: Wrong treatment. This is microcytic anemia from iron deficiency, not macrocytic anemia from B12 deficiency.

treatments.name: Dietary changes alone without iron supplementation
treatments.result: INSUFFICIENT - established iron deficiency requires supplementation.
treatments.reasoning: Diet alone cannot correct established iron deficiency. Must use supplements. And still need GI workup!

treatments.name: Iron supplements without GI workup
treatments.result: INAPPROPRIATE - must evaluate for source of iron loss.
treatments.reasoning: DANGEROUS: Treating iron deficiency without finding the source could miss colon cancer. Patient is >50 - colonoscopy is mandatory.

consults.name: GI consult for colonoscopy/EGD
consults.result: GI consulted. Colonoscopy and EGD scheduled.
consults.reasoning: MANDATORY: Must evaluate for GI source of blood loss. Colon cancer is a must-not-miss diagnosis.

consults.name: Hematology consult
consults.result: Not needed for straightforward iron deficiency anemia.
consults.reasoning: Iron deficiency is managed by primary care/internist. Heme consult if unclear etiology or refractory.

consults.name: Gynecology consult for menorrhagia
consults.result: Consider after ruling out GI source. Can address heavy menses.
consults.reasoning: Menorrhagia should be addressed but does NOT obviate the need for GI workup in patient >50.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with urgent GI referral
disposition.result: Discharged with iron supplementation. Urgent GI referral for endoscopy within 2-4 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Can be managed outpatient if stable. GI workup is urgent but not emergent.

disposition.name: Hospital admission
disposition.result: Not needed - patient is hemodynamically stable.
disposition.reasoning: Outpatient management appropriate for stable patient with Hgb 8.2. No indication for admission.

disposition.name: Discharge without GI referral
disposition.reasoning: DANGEROUS: Cannot discharge iron deficiency anemia in patient >50 without ensuring GI workup. Could miss cancer.

actualDiagnosis.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia with Occult GI Bleeding - Requires Colonoscopy to Rule Out Malignancy
keyFindings: Classic IDA symptoms: fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, palpitations with activity
PICA (ice craving/pagophagia) - highly specific for iron deficiency
Risk factors: vegetarian diet, heavy menses
Physical exam: conjunctival pallor, glossitis, flow murmur, early koilonychia
Labs confirm IDA: Hgb 8.2, MCV 72 (microcytic), ferritin 8 (depleted stores), low iron, high TIBC
POSITIVE STOOL GUAIAC - occult GI bleeding source must be identified
No CHF findings despite prior MI: no orthopnea, no PND, no edema, normal JVP, normal BNP
teachingPoints: PICA IS HIGHLY SPECIFIC FOR IRON DEFICIENCY: Ice craving (pagophagia) is almost diagnostic. Always ask about unusual cravings.
IRON DEFICIENCY IN ADULTS OVER 50 = GI WORKUP MANDATORY: You must rule out colon cancer. Even if patient has 'obvious' cause like heavy menses, still scope them.
FERRITIN IS THE MOST USEFUL SINGLE TEST: Low ferritin (<15-30) is specific for iron deficiency. But ferritin is an acute phase reactant - can be falsely normal with inflammation.

MCV HELPS CATEGORIZE ANEMIA: Low MCV (microcytic) = iron deficiency, thalassemia, or chronic disease. Normal MCV = early iron deficiency, chronic disease, renal disease. High MCV = B12/folate deficiency, liver disease.
FLOW MURMUR WITH ANEMIA: High cardiac output and decreased blood viscosity cause soft systolic murmurs across normal valves. Not pathologic.
DON'T FORGET THE RECTAL: Positive stool guaiac in patient with IDA clinches the need for GI workup.
IRON REPLACEMENT TAKES TIME: Expect reticulocyte response in 1-2 weeks, hemoglobin to rise 1-2 g/dL per month. Full repletion takes 3-6 months.

guidelineReferences.name: BSG Guidelines for Management of Iron Deficiency Anaemia 2021
keyPoints: Bidirectional GI workup (EGD + colonoscopy) for all men and postmenopausal women with IDA
Premenopausal women need GI workup if GI symptoms, family history, or refractory to iron
Ferritin <15 μg/L is diagnostic; <30 μg/L in inflammation
IV iron if oral not tolerated or rapid repletion needed
Celiac serology should be checked in all patients with IDA

guidelineReferences.name: AGA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Management of IDA 2020
keyPoints: Bidirectional endoscopy recommended for patients ≥50 with IDA
Oral iron: 30-60mg elemental iron every other day may improve absorption
IV iron for intolerance, malabsorption, or inflammatory conditions
Repeat Hgb in 2-4 weeks; ferritin at 3 months
Capsule endoscopy if bidirectional endoscopy negative

closureScript.diagnosis: Ms. Smith, we've found the cause of your fatigue. You're anemic - your blood count is low. Specifically, you have iron deficiency anemia. That explains everything: the tiredness, the shortness of breath, and even those ice cravings - craving ice is actually a classic sign of iron deficiency! Your vegetarian diet and heavy periods both contribute to losing iron faster than you're taking it in.
closureScript.plan: We're going to start you on iron supplements today - you'll take them every day with some vitamin C to help absorption. But here's the important part: because you're over 50 and your stool test shows a tiny bit of blood, we need you to have a colonoscopy. I know that sounds alarming, but it's the responsible thing to do. Iron deficiency in someone your age needs to be checked out to make sure nothing else is going on.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Iron pills can cause constipation and make your stool look black - that's normal. But call if you see bright red blood in your stool, feel dizzy or like you might faint, or have severe belly pain.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I want to reassure you that this doesn't mean we think you have cancer. In fact, the heavy periods and vegetarian diet probably explain your anemia completely. But part of taking good care of you is making absolutely sure we haven't missed anything. And once we've done the colonoscopy and it comes back normal, we'll all be able to breathe easier.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the hemoglobin was critically low?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same IDA but hemoglobin 5.2 g/dL with chest pain and dyspnea at rest

chiefComplaint.original: I've been so tired for months and can't catch my breath
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm extremely weak, short of breath at rest, and having chest pressure

feedback.correct: Symptomatic anemia with Hgb <7 (or <8 with cardiac disease) = transfuse. This patient has cardiac symptoms from anemia, needs admission, transfusion, and cardiac monitoring. Iron replacement is still needed but transfusion is immediate priority.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of transfusion need. What triggers transfusion?
feedback.incorrect: Transfusion thresholds: Hgb <7 for most patients, <8 for cardiac disease. Symptoms matter too - dyspnea at rest, angina, hemodynamic instability all indicate need for transfusion regardless of number. Don't just give iron and send home a symptomatic patient.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Mild/moderate IDA = outpatient iron replacement. Severe symptomatic IDA = admission, transfusion, cardiac monitoring. Symptoms and hemodynamic status drive decision, not just hemoglobin number.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were neurological symptoms with high MCV?
whatIfScenarios.description: Fatigue with numbness/tingling in hands and feet, unsteady gait, MCV 118

chiefComplaint.original: I've been so tired for months and can't catch my breath
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm exhausted, my hands and feet are numb, and I've been stumbling when I walk

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: B12 deficiency with subacute combined degeneration

feedback.correct: Macrocytic anemia + neurological symptoms = B12 DEFICIENCY. Subacute combined degeneration (posterior columns + corticospinal tracts). START B12 IMMEDIATELY - neuro damage can be IRREVERSIBLE. Check B12 and methylmalonic acid but don't wait for results to treat.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of B12 deficiency. What makes the neuro symptoms so urgent?
feedback.incorrect: B12 deficiency causes neurological damage BEFORE anemia becomes severe. Subacute combined degeneration: paresthesias, ataxia, spasticity. Can be IRREVERSIBLE if not treated promptly. Start B12 replacement immediately when clinically suspected - don't wait for results.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Iron deficiency = microcytic anemia, no neuro findings. B12 deficiency = macrocytic anemia, neurological involvement. Folate deficiency = macrocytic but NO neuro symptoms. The neurological findings distinguish B12 from folate.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if all cell lines were low (pancytopenia)?
whatIfScenarios.description: Fatigue with low hemoglobin, low WBC, and low platelets

chiefComplaint.original: I've been so tired for months and can't catch my breath
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm exhausted, I've had recurrent infections, and I bruise easily

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Bone marrow pathology (infiltration, aplasia, or MDS)
shouldRise: Need for hematology consult and bone marrow biopsy

feedback.correct: Pancytopenia = bone marrow problem. Differential: aplastic anemia, MDS, leukemia (aleukemic), marrow infiltration (lymphoma, mets, fibrosis), severe B12/folate deficiency. Needs peripheral smear review and BONE MARROW BIOPSY. Hematology consult.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of bone marrow pathology. What workup is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Pancytopenia (low RBC, WBC, AND platelets) = bone marrow failure or infiltration. Causes: aplastic anemia, MDS, leukemia, marrow infiltration, severe B12/folate deficiency. Workup: peripheral smear (blasts? dysplasia?), reticulocyte count, B12/folate, bone marrow biopsy is usually required.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Isolated anemia = often nutritional or blood loss, outpatient workup. Pancytopenia = bone marrow pathology, needs hematology and likely bone marrow biopsy. The involvement of multiple cell lines changes the differential entirely.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

sickle-cell-disease-primary.name: Patient
sickle-cell-disease-primary.chiefComplaint: Patient with known sickle cell disease presenting with severe bone pain
keyFindings: HbS polymerization under low O2 → sickle-shaped RBCs → vaso-occlusion + hemolysis
Vaso-occlusive crisis: severe pain, triggered by dehydration, infection, cold, hypoxia
Acute chest syndrome: chest pain + new infiltrate + fever — leading cause of death in adults with SCD
Functional asplenia by age 5 → increased encapsulated organism risk (Strep pneumo, H. flu, N. meningitidis)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Sickle Cell Disease
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup

questions.stem: A 35-year-old presents with petechiae, confusion, renal insufficiency, fever, and platelets of 18,000 with schistocytes on smear. What is the most critical immediate intervention?

options.text: Emergent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) — do not wait for ADAMTS13 results
options.explanation: This pentad (thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever) is classic TTP. Per ASH guidelines, plasma exchange must be initiated IMMEDIATELY — mortality without treatment exceeds 90%. Do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 activity levels; start treatment on clinical suspicion.

options.text: Platelet transfusion to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: Platelet transfusion in TTP is CONTRAINDICATED (relative) as it can fuel thrombotic microangiopathy and worsen organ damage. Platelets are consumed in microvascular thrombi, not bleeding.

TTP is distinguished from DIC by NORMAL coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen). DIC shows prolonged PT/PTT and low fibrinogen. Treatment differs fundamentally — TTP requires plasmapheresis, not anticoagulation.

options.text: Obtain ADAMTS13 activity level and treat based on results
options.explanation: ADAMTS13 testing takes 2-3 days. With suspected TTP, mortality approaches 90% without treatment. Plasma exchange must begin on clinical suspicion while awaiting confirmatory testing.

acute-leukemia-primary.name: Patient
acute-leukemia-primary.chiefComplaint: Child with fatigue, bruising, and pancytopenia on CBC
keyFindings: ALL: most common childhood cancer (peak 2-5 years), lymphoblasts, TdT+, PAS+
AML: more common in adults, myeloblasts, Auer rods pathognomonic, MPO+
APL (AML-M3): t(15;17), DIC presentation, treat with ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) — medical emergency
ALL treatment: induction (vincristine, prednisone, asparaginase), CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal methotrexate)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Acute Leukemia: ALL vs AML
Delayed diagnosis
Inadequate workup
